items.md: <naikassociates110@gmail.com>
# Indian Standard
items.value: Indian Standard

items.md: # ASSESSMENT OF NOISE WITH RESPECT TO COMMUNITY RESPONSE
items.value: ASSESSMENT OF NOISE WITH RESPECT TO COMMUNITY RESPONSE

items.md: ## 0. FOREWORD
items.value: 0. FOREWORD

items.md: **0.1** This Indian Standard was adopted by the Indian Standards Institution on 25 August 1981, after the draft finalized by the Acoustics Sectional Committee had been approved by the Electronics and Telecommunication Division Council.
items.value: 0.1 This Indian Standard was adopted by the Indian Standards Institution on 25 August 1981, after the draft finalized by the Acoustics Sectional Committee had been approved by the Electronics and Telecommunication Division Council.

items.md: **0.2** The reduction or limitation of noise is of increasing general importance. This standard suggests methods for measuring and rating noise in residential, industrial and traffic areas with respect to their interference with rest, working efficiency, social activities and tranquility.
items.value: 0.2 The reduction or limitation of noise is of increasing general importance. This standard suggests methods for measuring and rating noise in residential, industrial and traffic areas with respect to their interference with rest, working efficiency, social activities and tranquility.

items.md: **0.2.1** Besides noise there may be other factors in connection with sound production and radiation, for example, mechanical vibrations, which also give rise to annoyance in particular situations and this makes the assessment more complex. No general method exists at present to take account of these factors, and the application of numbers and corrections, other than those described in this standard, may be desirable in some cases.
items.value: 0.2.1 Besides noise there may be other factors in connection with sound production and radiation, for example, mechanical vibrations, which also give rise to annoyance in particular situations and this makes the assessment more complex. No general method exists at present to take account of these factors, and the application of numbers and corrections, other than those described in this standard, may be desirable in some cases.

items.md: **0.2.2** The method given for rating noises with respect to community response forms a basis on which limits for noises in various situations may be set by the competent authorities.
items.value: 0.2.2 The method given for rating noises with respect to community response forms a basis on which limits for noises in various situations may be set by the competent authorities.

items.md: **0.2.3** The method of rating involves the measurement of the A-weighted sound level in decibels \[ commonly called dB (A) ].
items.value: 0.2.3 The method of rating involves the measurement of the A-weighted sound level in decibels [ commonly called dB (A) ].

items.md: **0.2.4** Where corrective measures are required, a frequency analysis may be necessary. The resulting data may be compared with noise rating curves, for instance the NR-curves, in order to identify the intrusive frequency bands. This more elaborate procedure is described in Appendix A.
items.value: 0.2.4 Where corrective measures are required, a frequency analysis may be necessary. The resulting data may be compared with noise rating curves, for instance the NR-curves, in order to identify the intrusive frequency bands. This more elaborate procedure is described in Appendix A.

items.md: **0.3** In the preparation of this standard, assistance has been derived from ISO/R 1996-1971 'Assessment of noise with respect to community response' issued by the International Organization for Standardization.
items.value: 0.3 In the preparation of this standard, assistance has been derived from ISO/R 1996-1971 'Assessment of noise with respect to community response' issued by the International Organization for Standardization.

items.md: **0.4** In reporting the result of a test made in accordance with this standard, if the final value, observed or calculated, is to be rounded off, it shall be done in accordance with IS : 2-1960\*.
items.value: 0.4 In reporting the result of a test made in accordance with this standard, if the final value, observed or calculated, is to be rounded off, it shall be done in accordance with IS : 2-1960*.

items.md: \*Rules for rounding off numerical values (*revised*).
items.value: *Rules for rounding off numerical values (revised).

items.value: Free Standard provided by BIS via BSB Edge Private Limited to Krupaben Brahmbhatt - SURAT(

items.md: <naikassociates110@gmail.com>
# 1. SCOPE
items.value: 1. SCOPE

items.md: **1.1** This standard is intended as a guide to the measurement of the acceptability of noise in communities. It specifies a method for the measurement of noise, the application of corrections to the measured levels (according to duration, spectrum character and peak factor), and a comparison of the corrected levels with a noise criterion which takes account of various environmental factors.
items.value: 1.1 This standard is intended as a guide to the measurement of the acceptability of noise in communities. It specifies a method for the measurement of noise, the application of corrections to the measured levels (according to duration, spectrum character and peak factor), and a comparison of the corrected levels with a noise criterion which takes account of various environmental factors.

items.md: # 2. TERMINOLOGY
items.value: 2. TERMINOLOGY

items.md: **2.1** For the purpose of this standard, the terms and definitions given in IS : 1885 (Part III)\* shall apply.
items.value: 2.1 For the purpose of this standard, the terms and definitions given in IS : 1885 (Part III)* shall apply.

items.md: # 3. MEASURING EQUIPMENT
items.value: 3. MEASURING EQUIPMENT

items.md: **3.1** The measurements should be made with a sound level meter as specified in IS : 9779-1981†. The A-weighting network and fast response should be used. The sound level should be measured at the place and time of the annoyance.
items.value: 3.1 The measurements should be made with a sound level meter as specified in IS : 9779-1981†. The A-weighting network and fast response should be used. The sound level should be measured at the place and time of the annoyance.

items.md: Note — Other measuring equipment including, for example, a level recorder or tape recorder, may be used if the overall performance conforms with the characteristics of a sound level meter with A-weighting network and with fast response.
items.value: Note — Other measuring equipment including, for example, a level recorder or tape recorder, may be used if the overall performance conforms with the characteristics of a sound level meter with A-weighting network and with fast response.

items.md: # 4. CONDITIONS OF MEASUREMENT
items.value: 4. CONDITIONS OF MEASUREMENT

items.md: **4.1** Outdoor measurements should be made at 1·2 to 1·5 m above the ground and, if practical, at least 3·5 m from walls, buildings or other sound reflecting structures. When circumstances indicate, measurements may be made at greater heights and closer to the wall (for example 0·5 m in front of an open window), provided this is specified and taken into consideration.
items.value: 4.1 Outdoor measurements should be made at 1·2 to 1·5 m above the ground and, if practical, at least 3·5 m from walls, buildings or other sound reflecting structures. When circumstances indicate, measurements may be made at greater heights and closer to the wall (for example 0·5 m in front of an open window), provided this is specified and taken into consideration.

items.md: Note 1 — Care should be taken to avoid the influence of unwanted sound signals on the result, for example noise of wind on the microphone of the measuring equipment, noise from electrical interference or noise from extraneous sources.
items.value: Note 1 — Care should be taken to avoid the influence of unwanted sound signals on the result, for example noise of wind on the microphone of the measuring equipment, noise from electrical interference or noise from extraneous sources.

items.md: Note 2 — When the noise source is distant, the measured sound level may depend significantly on climatic conditions. It is recommended that extreme conditions be avoided. If possible, a typical value and an indication of the range of variation should be obtained.
items.value: Note 2 — When the noise source is distant, the measured sound level may depend significantly on climatic conditions. It is recommended that extreme conditions be avoided. If possible, a typical value and an indication of the range of variation should be obtained.

items.md: **4.2** Indoor measurements should be made at a distance of at least 1 m from the walls, 1·2 to 1·5 m above the floor, and about 1·5 m from the window(s). In order to reduce disturbances from standing waves, the sound levels measured indoors should be averaged over $\pm$ 0·5 m of each of at least 3 positions. This is especially important when measuring low-frequency noise. The arithmetic average of the readings determines the value to be taken.
items.value: 4.2 Indoor measurements should be made at a distance of at least 1 m from the walls, 1·2 to 1·5 m above the floor, and about 1·5 m from the window(s). In order to reduce disturbances from standing waves, the sound levels measured indoors should be averaged over $\pm$ 0·5 m of each of at least 3 positions. This is especially important when measuring low-frequency noise. The arithmetic average of the readings determines the value to be taken.

items.md:
\*Electrotechnical vocabulary: Part III Acoustics.
†Sound level meters (Superseding IS : 3931-1966 and IS : 3932-1966).
items.value:
*Electrotechnical vocabulary: Part III Acoustics.
†Sound level meters (Superseding IS : 3931-1966 and IS : 3932-1966).

**IS : 9989 - 1981**

items.value: Free Standard provided by BIS via BSB Edge Private Limited to Krupaben Brahmbhatt - SURAT(

items.md: <naikassociates110@gmail.com>
**4.2.1** The measurements should generally be made with windows closed. If the room is regularly used with open windows, measurements should also be made under this condition.
items.value: 4.2.1 The measurements should generally be made with windows closed. If the room is regularly used with open windows, measurements should also be made under this condition.

items.md: **4.2.2** If the noise is not steady, the level and duration of the noise must be determined; if necessary, records of the level may be made. The period of time in which the time history of the sound level is observed must be chosen according to the character of the variations of the noise. If possible, the period should cover more than one typical variation cycle.
items.value: 4.2.2 If the noise is not steady, the level and duration of the noise must be determined; if necessary, records of the level may be made. The period of time in which the time history of the sound level is observed must be chosen according to the character of the variations of the noise. If possible, the period should cover more than one typical variation cycle.

items.md: # 5. DETERMINATION OF THE RATING SOUND LEVEL $L\_r$
items.value: 5. DETERMINATION OF THE RATING SOUND LEVEL $L_r$

items.md: **5.1** In many cases, corrections to the measured sound level, $L\_A$, are needed to obtain a better estimate of the community response to the noise. These corrections are dependent on the character of the noise with respect to peak factor, spectrum character, duration and fluctuation. The sum of $L\_A$ and possible corrections is termed the rating sound level, $L\_r$, that is the sound level of a steady noise without impulsive character or pure tones which is assumed to cause the same community response as the measured noise.
items.value: 5.1 In many cases, corrections to the measured sound level, $L_A$, are needed to obtain a better estimate of the community response to the noise. These corrections are dependent on the character of the noise with respect to peak factor, spectrum character, duration and fluctuation. The sum of $L_A$ and possible corrections is termed the rating sound level, $L_r$, that is the sound level of a steady noise without impulsive character or pure tones which is assumed to cause the same community response as the measured noise.

items.md: **5.2** The procedure is as follows:
items.value: 5.2 The procedure is as follows:

items.md: **5.2.1** Steady noise (such as rain noise) without impulsive character or audible tones is rated by the sound level $L\_A$ in dB(A), measured by means of the sound level meter.
items.value: 5.2.1 Steady noise (such as rain noise) without impulsive character or audible tones is rated by the sound level $L_A$ in dB(A), measured by means of the sound level meter.

items.md: **5.2.2** Steady noise with an impulsive character (such as hammering or riveting) or with discrete noise impulses is rated by the sound level $L\_A$ in dB(A) plus the correction given in Table 1, first entry.
items.value: 5.2.2 Steady noise with an impulsive character (such as hammering or riveting) or with discrete noise impulses is rated by the sound level $L_A$ in dB(A) plus the correction given in Table 1, first entry.

items.md: **5.2.2.1** The reading to be taken is the average of the maximum deflections of the pointer.
items.value: 5.2.2.1 The reading to be taken is the average of the maximum deflections of the pointer.

items.md: NOTE 1 — Other techniques for measuring and rating impulsive noise may become appropriate, especially when recommendations for suitable measuring instruments are published.
items.value: NOTE 1 — Other techniques for measuring and rating impulsive noise may become appropriate, especially when recommendations for suitable measuring instruments are published.

items.md: NOTE 2 — If the sound level varies over a large range, the procedure described in **5.2.5** should be used.
items.value: NOTE 2 — If the sound level varies over a large range, the procedure described in 5.2.5 should be used.

items.md: **5.2.3** Steady noise which contains audible tone components (for example whine, screech or hum) is rated by the sound level $L\_A$ in dB(A) plus the correction given in Table 1, second entry.
items.value: 5.2.3 Steady noise which contains audible tone components (for example whine, screech or hum) is rated by the sound level $L_A$ in dB(A) plus the correction given in Table 1, second entry.

items.md: **5.2.4** If the noise is interrupted by pauses (for example, almost unchanging factory noise lasting for several hours followed by a pause), a correction according to Table 1, third entry, should be applied to the sound level $L\_A$ to take account of the reduced duration of the noise.
items.value: 5.2.4 If the noise is interrupted by pauses (for example, almost unchanging factory noise lasting for several hours followed by a pause), a correction according to Table 1, third entry, should be applied to the sound level $L_A$ to take account of the reduced duration of the noise.

items.md: <naikassociates110@gmail.com>
## TABLE 1 CORRECTIONS TO THE MEASURED SOUND LEVEL IN dB(A)
items.value: TABLE 1 CORRECTIONS TO THE MEASURED SOUND LEVEL IN dB(A)

items.md:
| CHARACTERISTIC FEATURES OF THE NOISE                                                      | CHARACTERISTIC FEATURES OF THE NOISE                   | CORRECTIONdB (A) |
| ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------- |
| Peak factor                                                                               | Impulsive noise ( for example, from hammering )        | + 5              |
| Spectrum character                                                                        | Audible tone components present ( for example, whine ) | + 5              |
| Duration of the noise with sound level $L\_A$ as a percentage of the relevant time period | Between: 100 and 56                                    | 0                |
|                                                                                           | Between: 56 and 18                                     | — 5              |
|                                                                                           | Between: 18 and 6                                      | — 10             |
|                                                                                           | Between: 6 and 1·8                                     | — 15             |
|                                                                                           | Between: 1·8 and 0·6                                   | — 20             |
|                                                                                           | Between: 0·6 and 0·2                                   | — 25             |
|                                                                                           | Less than 0·2                                          | — 30             |
rows: Impulsive noise ( for example, from hammering )
Audible tone components present ( for example, whine )
Duration of the noise with sound level $L_A$ as a percentage of the relevant time period

items.md: **5.2.4.1** The duration of the noise should be reckoned over a relevant time period which may be set according to the specifications by local authorities, for example, the most unfavourable 8 consecutive hours during daytime and the most unfavourable half-hour of the evening or the night. For noise during the night, it may also be advantageous to set an absolute limit for the sound level.
items.value: 5.2.4.1 The duration of the noise should be reckoned over a relevant time period which may be set according to the specifications by local authorities, for example, the most unfavourable 8 consecutive hours during daytime and the most unfavourable half-hour of the evening or the night. For noise during the night, it may also be advantageous to set an absolute limit for the sound level.

items.md: NOTE 1 — The time limits for day, evening, and night may vary in different countries and may be defined by local authorities according to the way in which the ambient traffic noise varies and according to the habits of people. ( For instance, in some areas day lasts from 06·00 until 18·00, evening from 18·00 until midnight, and night from midnight until 06·00 ).
items.value: NOTE 1 — The time limits for day, evening, and night may vary in different countries and may be defined by local authorities according to the way in which the ambient traffic noise varies and according to the habits of people. ( For instance, in some areas day lasts from 06·00 until 18·00, evening from 18·00 until midnight, and night from midnight until 06·00 ).

items.md: NOTE 2 — By specifying a shorter period or an absolute limit during evening and night, the influence of noises with high levels and short duration is emphasized. Such noise may be disturbing for sleep.
items.value: NOTE 2 — By specifying a shorter period or an absolute limit during evening and night, the influence of noises with high levels and short duration is emphasized. Such noise may be disturbing for sleep.

items.md: NOTE 3 — If a particular noise source is to be considered at weekends, measurements must take into account the circumstances of the weekend, for example, by measuring the background noise at the relevant time.
items.value: NOTE 3 — If a particular noise source is to be considered at weekends, measurements must take into account the circumstances of the weekend, for example, by measuring the background noise at the relevant time.

items.md: **5.2.5** If the noise varies with time in a more complicated manner than is appropriate for the use of Table 1, the equivalent sound level $L\_{eq}$ should be obtained, for example, from a statistical analysis of the time history of the A-weighted sound level. The corrections in Table 1 for peak factor or noise spectrum character should also be applied when appropriate.
items.value: 5.2.5 If the noise varies with time in a more complicated manner than is appropriate for the use of Table 1, the equivalent sound level $L_{eq}$ should be obtained, for example, from a statistical analysis of the time history of the A-weighted sound level. The corrections in Table 1 for peak factor or noise spectrum character should also be applied when appropriate.

items.md: **5.2.5.1** The statistical analysis can be based on analogue or digital recordings of the sound level. For estimating purposes, it may in some cases be sufficient to determine the statistical distribution by observing the sound level meter readings at intervals of time by a sampling technique.
items.value: 5.2.5.1 The statistical analysis can be based on analogue or digital recordings of the sound level. For estimating purposes, it may in some cases be sufficient to determine the statistical distribution by observing the sound level meter readings at intervals of time by a sampling technique.

items.value: $L_i$ = is the sound level in dB(A) corresponding to the class-midpoint of the class $i$ [ for class intervals not greater then 5 dB(A) the arithmetic means can be used; for larger intervals logarithmic averaging should be used ];

items.md:
**5.3** Hence, the rating sound level is determined as follows:
a) for noises of constant level, by
$L\_r = L\_A + 5$ when the noise is impulsive or when it contains audible tone components or both + correction for duration when the noise is intermittent; and
b) for noises of fluctuating level, by
$L\_r = L\_{eq} + 5$ when the noise is impulsive or when it contains audible tone components or both.
items.value:
5.3 Hence, the rating sound level is determined as follows:
a) for noises of constant level, by
$L_r = L_A + 5$ when the noise is impulsive or when it contains audible tone components or both + correction for duration when the noise is intermittent; and
b) for noises of fluctuating level, by
$L_r = L_{eq} + 5$ when the noise is impulsive or when it contains audible tone components or both.

items.md: # 6. NOISE CRITERIA ( *see* Appendix B )
items.value: 6. NOISE CRITERIA ( see Appendix B )

items.md: **6.1** In general, a noise is liable to provoke complaints whenever its level exceeds by a certain margin that of the pre-existing background noise, or when it attains a certain absolute level.
items.value: 6.1 In general, a noise is liable to provoke complaints whenever its level exceeds by a certain margin that of the pre-existing background noise, or when it attains a certain absolute level.

items.md: **6.1.1** The method of rating noise is based on a comparison of the rating sound level with a criterion level which takes various features of the environment into account. The criterion is related to the pre-existing background level, either fixed for a certain zone in general or directly measured for special cases.
items.value: 6.1.1 The method of rating noise is based on a comparison of the rating sound level with a criterion level which takes various features of the environment into account. The criterion is related to the pre-existing background level, either fixed for a certain zone in general or directly measured for special cases.

items.md: **6.1.2** The method for deriving a criterion for rating noise in general ( for example, for zoning purposes ) is given in **6.2**; the method for rating
items.value: 6.1.2 The method for deriving a criterion for rating noise in general ( for example, for zoning purposes ) is given in 6.2; the method for rating

items.md: ### 6.2 Noise Criteria in General
items.value: 6.2 Noise Criteria in General

items.md: **6.2.1** Noise criteria in general, especially for the purpose of zoning, can be derived from one basic value by adding corrections for time of day and corrections for the different types of district.
items.value: 6.2.1 Noise criteria in general, especially for the purpose of zoning, can be derived from one basic value by adding corrections for time of day and corrections for the different types of district.

items.md: **6.2.1.1** The basic value for a country has to be established according to the living habits of the people.
items.value: 6.2.1.1 The basic value for a country has to be established according to the living habits of the people.

items.md: NOTE — The basic criterion for residential premises should usually be in the range of 35 to 45 dB(A) for outdoor noise as recommended by ISO.
items.value: NOTE — The basic criterion for residential premises should usually be in the range of 35 to 45 dB(A) for outdoor noise as recommended by ISO.

items.md: **6.2.2** The corrections to the basic criterion for different times of day are given in Table 2.
items.value: 6.2.2 The corrections to the basic criterion for different times of day are given in Table 2.

items.md: **TABLE 2 CONNECTIONS TO BASIC CRITERION FOR DIFFERENT TIMES OF DAY**

| ----------- | ------------------------------------ |
| Day         | 0                                    |
| Evening     | - 5                                  |
| Night       | - 10 to - 15                         |

**6.2.3.1** Local experience in different countries will lead to different definitions of the relevant types of zones, taking into account existing laws or prescriptions.
items.value: 6.2.3.1 Local experience in different countries will lead to different definitions of the relevant types of zones, taking into account existing laws or prescriptions.

items.md: ### 6.3 Special Cases
items.value: 6.3 Special Cases

items.md: **6.3.1** For rating noise in special cases, for example in case of complaints of a certain noise source at a certain place, the background noise level serves as the criterion.
items.value: 6.3.1 For rating noise in special cases, for example in case of complaints of a certain noise source at a certain place, the background noise level serves as the criterion.

items.md: **6.3.2** The background (ambient) noise level is the mean minimum sound level at the relevant place and time in the absence of the noise which is alleged to be offending. It should be obtained by observing the pointer of the sound level meter and by reading the lowest level which is repeated several times (mean minimum). When statistical analysis of the sound level is used, the background noise level should be taken as that level which is exceeded for 95 percent of the observation time.
items.value: 6.3.2 The background (ambient) noise level is the mean minimum sound level at the relevant place and time in the absence of the noise which is alleged to be offending. It should be obtained by observing the pointer of the sound level meter and by reading the lowest level which is repeated several times (mean minimum). When statistical analysis of the sound level is used, the background noise level should be taken as that level which is exceeded for 95 percent of the observation time.

items.md: NOTE 1 — The background noise level includes the influence of the type of district, the season and the time of day, and no correction has to be used. It serves in the same way for assessment of noise outside or inside a building, with windows open or closed, provided it is measured under the same conditions as the noise.
items.value: NOTE 1 — The background noise level includes the influence of the type of district, the season and the time of day, and no correction has to be used. It serves in the same way for assessment of noise outside or inside a building, with windows open or closed, provided it is measured under the same conditions as the noise.

items.md: NOTE 2 — To prevent a creeping (gradually increasing) background noise level, it may be useful to compare the measured background level with the general criterion derived according to **6.2** for the relevant district and time.
items.value: NOTE 2 — To prevent a creeping (gradually increasing) background noise level, it may be useful to compare the measured background level with the general criterion derived according to 6.2 for the relevant district and time.

items.md: ## 7. ASSESSMENT OF NOISE WITH RESPECT TO COMMUNITY RESPONSE
items.value: 7. ASSESSMENT OF NOISE WITH RESPECT TO COMMUNITY RESPONSE

items.md: **7.1** In order to assess the noise with respect to the expected community response, the rating sound level as obtained according to **5** should be compared with the criterion value given in **6.2** or **6.3**.
items.value: 7.1 In order to assess the noise with respect to the expected community response, the rating sound level as obtained according to 5 should be compared with the criterion value given in 6.2 or 6.3.

items.md: **7.2** If the rating sound level exceeds the criterion value, the noise is likely to evoke response from the community. Differences of 5 dB(A) or less are of marginal significance, complaints may certainly be expected if the difference reaches 10 dB(A) or more. An estimate of the public reaction which may be obtained where the rating sound level exceeds the criterion value by a certain amount is given in Appendix C.
items.value: 7.2 If the rating sound level exceeds the criterion value, the noise is likely to evoke response from the community. Differences of 5 dB(A) or less are of marginal significance, complaints may certainly be expected if the difference reaches 10 dB(A) or more. An estimate of the public reaction which may be obtained where the rating sound level exceeds the criterion value by a certain amount is given in Appendix C.

items.md: ## 8. STATEMENTS IN THE REPORT
items.value: 8. STATEMENTS IN THE REPORT

items.md: **8.1** The report shall state:
items.value: 8.1 The report shall state:

items.md: a) the measured sound level $L\_A$ in dB(A);
items.value: a) the measured sound level $L_A$ in dB(A);

items.md: b) the duration of the noise or, for varying levels, the statistical distribution;
items.value: b) the duration of the noise or, for varying levels, the statistical distribution;

items.md: c) operating conditions of the noise source and weather conditions (where applicable);
items.value: c) operating conditions of the noise source and weather conditions (where applicable);

# APPENDIX A
items.value: APPENDIX A

items.md: ## ( Clause 0.2.4 )
items.value: ( Clause 0.2.4 )

items.md: ## FREQUENCY ANALYSIS
items.value: FREQUENCY ANALYSIS

items.md: **A-1.** The recommended method of rating, based on measurements of the A-weighted sound level, is given in the body of this standard. However, a frequency analysis of the noise will in some cases be valuable for rating purposes and is essential if corrective measures to reduce the noise nuisance are to be evaluated. In this case, a set of noise rating curves (NR-curves), with which the measured spectrum of the noise can be compared, can be employed. This makes an identification of the intrusive frequency bands possible.
items.value: A-1. The recommended method of rating, based on measurements of the A-weighted sound level, is given in the body of this standard. However, a frequency analysis of the noise will in some cases be valuable for rating purposes and is essential if corrective measures to reduce the noise nuisance are to be evaluated. In this case, a set of noise rating curves (NR-curves), with which the measured spectrum of the noise can be compared, can be employed. This makes an identification of the intrusive frequency bands possible.

items.md: **A-2.** The NR-curves are given in Fig. 1 and the octave band pressure levels corresponding to the curves are tabulated in Table 4.
items.value: A-2. The NR-curves are given in Fig. 1 and the octave band pressure levels corresponding to the curves are tabulated in Table 4.

items.md: **A-3.** An octave band analysis of the noise in the range 31.5 to 8 000 Hz (centre-frequencies) should be made with filters according to IS : 6964-1973\*. These octave band pressure levels shall be corrected, if necessary, according to **5**. To each corrected band pressure level an NR-number, in accordance with Table 4 or Fig. 1, shall be assigned. These numbers can be compared with a criterion in NR-numbers, the numerical value of which may be taken as 5 lower than the criterion according to **7** in dB(A).
items.value: A-3. An octave band analysis of the noise in the range 31.5 to 8 000 Hz (centre-frequencies) should be made with filters according to IS : 6964-1973*. These octave band pressure levels shall be corrected, if necessary, according to 5. To each corrected band pressure level an NR-number, in accordance with Table 4 or Fig. 1, shall be assigned. These numbers can be compared with a criterion in NR-numbers, the numerical value of which may be taken as 5 lower than the criterion according to 7 in dB(A).

items.md: \*Octave, half-octave and third-octave band filters for analysis of sound and vibrations.
items.value: *Octave, half-octave and third-octave band filters for analysis of sound and vibrations.

items.md: ### RATING INDOOR NOISE
items.value: RATING INDOOR NOISE

items.md: **B-1.** General criterion values for rating noise inside residential premises can be derived from those for outdoor noise specified in **6.2**, by adding corrections according to the decrease in sound level from outdoors to indoors with open and closed windows.
items.value: B-1. General criterion values for rating noise inside residential premises can be derived from those for outdoor noise specified in 6.2, by adding corrections according to the decrease in sound level from outdoors to indoors with open and closed windows.

items.md: **B-1.1** These corrections are given in Table 5.
